## [00:00 - 03:06]

Good morning, everyone. Welcome to New York and the beautiful St. Regis Hotel on this lovely spring day. My name is Pauline Bensevanger, but don't worry, I'm not the Bensevanger you'll be hearing from today. You each picked up a name tag when you came in this morning, and we ask that you hold on to it because it will be your ID card when you come back tomorrow. If any of you have any questions or need assistance, please come to me or one of our helpers. We have TJ Robinson, Hannah Lee, Nancy Lowsky, and Megan Lewis. So please feel free to come to us and hopefully we can help you. We also ask that you please turn off your cell phones. You can see we are videotaping this, and Gary will tell you something a little special about that, that you'll all be pleased to hear. So now it's my pleasure to introduce the man who is the most important person in my life, and I think by the end of this seminar he'll be an important person in your life as well. I'd like you to join me with a warm welcome for my husband Gary. Thank you. Thank you so much. That was a wonderful way to greet me on my way in. It was really I who should be applauding all of you because as I looked at the roster and see all of you in front of me now, we've got the greatest collection of direct marketing talent ever assembled in one room right here. So I applaud you. I know the DMA and other industry groups have more people at their conventions, but we've got the best right here. In fact, we have top direct marketing talent from every continent on the globe except for the one lone cold exception of Antarctica, where they have more penguins than people. And the whole continent is melting anyway, so I think they've got bigger wood things to worry about. I want to thank you Pauline for that very nice introduction. This marks the 40th year that I've been a copywriter and the 40th year that I'm married to Pauline. And there is a connection there and that Pauline was the inspiration for my greatest headline and offer ever. The headline was, Will You Marry Me? The offer was, Two Can Live As Cheaply As One, and I got a 100% response.

## [03:06 - 06:10]

I grew up right across the river, right across the East River in a rough and tumble section of Brooklyn called Crown Heights. And believe me, if I could make it from the rough streets of Crown Heights over here to the St. Regis talking to the likes of you, the preeminent talent in direct marketing today, there's nothing that can stop you from being or doing or achieving anything in your lives that you want to do. But why this room? You know, we could have had this seminar in any place and I wanted it here as a statement, a statement about you, about me, about the nature of aspiration. And that statement can be summed up in just four little words. Why not the best? Why should the best be reserved for others? I know, and maybe this is the Brooklyn enemy coming out. You know, the other boroughs are always second fiddle to Manhattan and you can take the guy out of Brooklyn, but you can't take the Brooklyn out of the guy. So maybe we're always thinking of the best and maybe some of you feel the same way that sometimes the best is reserved for other people. That's not so in a free society where we all have the chance to compete on an equal playing field and we are all from free societies. You can achieve the best, you can do the best, so why not strive for the best? That's a philosophy I always share with my clients when I'm doing a project. And as, you know, as Somerset Maugham, as a great writer, Somerset Maugham once said, it's a funny thing about life when you refuse to accept nothing but the best. You very often experience it. And so that statement is just more than a slogan for me. I try to apply that with my clients and everything that we do because there's no fun or great rewards long term in mediocrity, so let's take the time to do things right. Part of that is having a vision of what you'd like to do before each activity, whether it's in marketing or copywriting or just life in general, having a very clear outcome of what you'd like to experience in any major activity. So in other words, a breakthrough advertising campaign does not start writing headlines, it does not start with your research, it really starts with an intention to make it so. In 500 BC, the great military genius Sun Tzu, the great general of China, wrote a treatise called The Art of War. And he said, and by the way, The Art of War is a book that's studied in every major military college in the world today. And he said, the battle is won even before it is engaged. And a modern day student of Sun Tzu has said, Bill Belichick, who is the winning coach in three of the last four Super Bowls, he said, the Super Bowl is won long before the players take the field. And in the same way, a direct meal package becomes the new control even before the rider's fingers touch the keyboard.

## [06:10 - 09:21]

The space ad becomes a breakthrough even before the headline is written. The new product is a breakthrough even before it's launched, if we have the intention to make it so. And in a similar fashion, the seminar becomes a life-changing experience even before it unfolds if we all work together to make it so. So what is my vision for this seminar then that I've thought through and would love you to experience? Well, my vision is tomorrow, late tomorrow afternoon, when your feet once again touch the sidewalks of New York after our last session together at the Rock. After our cocktail party tomorrow. I would like you to feel almost lighter than air because you are so excited about the ideas that you've learned here and that we've shared together. And can't wait to apply them in your life because I don't want this to be what I call a suntan seminar where you have a warm glow for three days and then it sort of fades away and you're back to wherever you are before. Because the only way this will be a value in your life is if you make notes of the good ideas that you hear and then put them into practice and we'll get into a lot of that as we move ahead. How to do that, the habits of winning. I'd like to use sports analogies and the great football coach, Vince Lombardi once said that everybody knows his famous quote, that winning isn't everything, it's the only thing. And I know many of us copywriters face that pressure when we have to write a winning package. But he also said a much less well known quote is that winning is a habit and so unfortunately is losing. And so one of the things that I'll be talking about is how to develop the habits of a winner in whatever field of life you are engaged. But since most of us here in marketing, how do we apply those winning habits to producing winning products. Again, getting back to the talent that is in this room. Imagine how wonderful that could be if we all shook hands tomorrow afternoon and formed a corporation where we were all even Stephen partners in a new direct marketing corporation. The talent in this room, and we have the extraordinary copywriter, superstar designers, best list brokers, the incredible entrepreneurs who have achieved mega million dollar businesses in direct marketing and on and on marketing managers. And the rule right here. So imagine if we could have a corporate, we would rule the direct marketing world for years if not decades. Of course, that's not going to happen because we all have our prior commitments. And I would nevertheless like to try to capture a little bit of that feeling. I'd like us all to relate to one another as members of an elite all star team and that we're all deserving of each other's mutual and kind respect just as it would be on an all star team.

## [09:21 - 12:22]

And if you are the all star team, that means I get to be the coach. I should be wearing a whistle. I guess if you misbehave, I'll make you do a lap around this beautiful room. But I mean that in another way as well and that the principles that I will be sharing are as powerful and as reliable as the laws of gravity or electricity. They don't depend on me applying them or you applying if you apply them, they will work. If you don't apply them, they won't work. And as I'll explain later, I was quite a mediocre copywriter really for the first that eight or nine years of my career until I learned a system of that I'm going to be sharing with you and a system that I've helped refine with some other. Very talented direct marketing people. And I say that it's not really humility, my saying that I really was a mediocre copywriter as you may have guessed. If you read my sales letters or the sales letters of other copywriters as a group, we are not burdened by false modesty. So when I tell you that I was mediocre for the first seven or eight years of my career, you can believe it. In fact, if anything, it might be a stretch on the upside where that's a compliment to what I was doing in those days. So the system that I'm going to teach you and the components really will apply irrespective of my personality or anyone else. And that is important because don't judge them by what I've done or not done in my life. Just as say a coach, a coach, Angelo Dundee, who was a great boxing trainer, he could not last in the ring with a top ranked heavyweight more than say 15 seconds. But he knew how to bring the best out of Muhammad Ali and make him the greatest. So look at me in that light. Don't judge me by what I may have done or not done. There are entrepreneurs in this room who have done incredible things. But we're not comparing anyone to each other, including me, because the only one that you should ever compete with in any activity is yourself. If you could be a little bit better next week than you were this week, well then you are succeeding. And that's how I view my career and ambition. I just try not to compete with anyone else because then I try to be more like them if I have too much of my focus on someone else. I want to be the best I can be and if I can improve 1% a week, I'm happy. So that's how we will relate to one another. Okay, so if I'm the coach, what are the pages from Gary's playbook? There we are. We'll get to explaining these as I go through the seminar. But just to give you an overview, one of the things that I just mentioned is 1% weekly improvement. You don't have to improve your abilities 50% in one week. That's too hard. It's too high a goal. What you want to do is just try to improve 1% per week in whatever you're doing. Whether you're playing the guitar or the piano or trying to be a better copywriter or a marketer,

## [12:22 - 15:28]

if you can increase your ability 1% per week, that means that in a year you're at least 50% better than you are today. In two years you're 100%. And over time that really compounds and you then do become a superstar. If you start out as a superstar, you become incredible at that rate of improvement. And it's very easy to do. One of the tools that I'm doing that is what I recommend is my add a day program. Every single day you should, no matter what else you'd have to get done in that day, read a good ad or at least a piece of a good ad. And so that you're always keeping that in your consciousness about what really great advertising is. I'll get more into that later. I'll also get into something called the Great Fisherman's Secret and how that can help you in marketing. The Benzenevaenga Persuasion Equation, which gets to that system that I told you about, is a very reliable system for taking direct response breakthroughs out of the realm of the occasional and haphazard and unpredictable and bringing the whole process into a very predictable and repeatable mechanism. Also, you'll learn the secret of master closers, the Pareto principle, and the important management tool also for marketers, where in any activity a very few number of factors will usually yield the vast number of results. It's normally known as the 80-20 rule. So what I'm going to be doing is through this 40-year career that I've enjoyed just isolating so that we don't get confused by all of the different techniques that we can apply. We're just going to identify the real few that will give you the outsized proportion of your results. We're going to be talking about the 80%, 90% just rely on doing a few things right, and that's what we're going to really put the focus on. We will talk about copyrights and marketers and designers. We want to help other people intensify, clarify, and then fulfill their desires. ## [18:32 - 21:32]

Wow, this is a friendly group that I never imagined. Okay. I think, okay, well, we're going to resume. Well, thank you. This is a friendly group that I never would have imagined. That's very nice. Thank you for welcoming everyone else. So as I mentioned before, as the Zen master might say, if you're going to hit a target, it pays to start looking at the target before you release your arrow. So what is advertising? And what I'm going to do here is touch upon some things that are all basic. Let's imagine that we are the world champions. I was about to say Yankees, but with a world champion at any sport, spring training is always good. Even the veteran superstars just get back on the fundamentals of the game. So we're going to touch on that very briefly to make sure we're all on the same page together. And then very quickly after that, take it to advanced levels from the foundation that we want to talk about. So I know some of you know this information, but I want to be sure everybody is on the same page. So we do have people who are superstars of the industry as well as copycubs, all in this one room. And we all need to start together. So okay, what is advertising? Well, many people may not realize this, but this very month is the... Well, actually I have a slide for this. I had a lot of fun putting this PowerPoint together, so you'll forgive me for silly slides for some of the things I want to talk about. But this is the 101st anniversary of the birth of modern advertising. Because it was in 1904, May of 1904, that a famous meeting took place. It took place between two of the three great founders of modern advertising. The man on the left is Albert Lasker, and this meeting took place with the man in the center, the first really brilliant copyrighted John E. Kennedy in 1904. Albert Lasker was the son of a prosperous German immigrant, and he could have almost had any career he wanted. He started out as a reporter in journalism, and he got the idea that he really liked advertising. He saw that with the right advertising, businesses flourished. They made their founders and key executives wealthy beyond the dreams of adorers, but in many cases the advertising didn't work, and he had no idea what are the factors that make it work when it does work, and what are the factors that don't work for those where it's failing. And he was searching all over the country for somebody who can give him a better idea about what the factors of great advertising are.

## [21:32 - 24:33]

And he found his way to the Lord and Thomas ad agency in Chicago in the early 1900s. He started at age 18, and he started out cleaning the spittoons in the office and saloon downstairs. And within two years he was the owner of the agency. He was such a go-getter. He was great in personal sales presentations. He could sort of chan the birds out of the trees. In fact, later he teamed up with Claude Hopkins, and Claude Hopkins said of him, there's nobody who could resist the persuasive power of Albert Lasker when Albert wanted you to do something. So he was very persuasive personally, but he had no idea of how to translate this personal magnetism into advertising. And he was in the office of the owner of the company at that time, and he was young. It was Mr. Thomas, and they were talking about business, and a bellman brought a note up and handed it to Mr. Thomas, who was the head of the ad agency. And the note said as follows, this is probably the first great direct mail letter, at least in terms of sorting modern advertising. The little note came from the man in the middle. It was a Canadian Mountie turned copywriter, and he wrote it as follows, Dear Mr. Thomas, I am in the saloon downstairs. I can tell you what advertising is. I know that you do not know. Now see, copyrighters always have this attitude that, you know, it will mean much to me to have you know, and it will mean much to you. If you wish to know what advertising is, send the word yes down by the bellboy, and he signed the letter. And this is very ad, this is not an urban myth, because Lasker recorded this exactly as it happened, and being a journalist, he said exactly, factually, how this meeting took place, and he saved that note. And Mr. Thomas said, what, the cranks who wander into that saloon downstairs. And so he was about to throw it, and Lasker though had great curiosity, he said, look, I'll meet with him, maybe he can teach us something, who knows. And so he and John E. Kennedy had a meeting, and Kennedy said, Mr. Lasker, I've heard about your reputation, you're really good at what you do, but you've been asking all the heads of the other agencies, what is good advertising, and I've heard, because you know, it's word in the industry is you haven't found a good answer yet. Well I have the answer, but let me ask you first, what do you, so you'll know that I'm giving you this answer, and it's not coming from anyone else, what do you think, let me test you, what do you think good advertising is? So he said, you know, I really have been trying to find out the answer to that, and as close as I can come to understand it, and maybe this is because I'm a journalist, I think advertising is news, news about a product. And John E. Kennedy said, no, no, news is a technique of presentation, that's not what advertising is.

## [24:33 - 27:40]

Advertising is salesmanship in print, and those three words were the foundation of modern advertising. In fact, when David Algarve wrote his book, Algarve on Advertising, great book, he said there's never been an improvement on that definition. I think there may be one slight improvement, which came along later, by the man on the right, the all-time greatest king of copywriters, Claude Hopkins, who said, there are different media, so to say, salesmanship in print, if it's on radio, that's not print, let's call great advertising multiplied salesmanship, because that's what it is. Now, I know that some of you, and this has been sort of the gospel of direct marketers, and we wish, you know, the other people on Madison Avenue, up and down these canyons, and Madison Avenue was this way, and Fifth Avenue was that way, there are thousands of agencies within this radius of two miles, yet very few of them know about this definition because they don't measure their advertising, they just run it on TV, and if it gets a good laugh from the person in the next cubicle, they think it's a big hit, and it's an entertaining ad. But those who measure their advertising, as we all do in this room, know the value of that definition. We want to sell more of our goods, we don't just want to win an award or applause, because what multiplied salesmanship is, is a single good salesperson able to talk to a thousand people at once, and so if you're really good at selling, look at the leverage that gets you, instead of knocking on a thousand doors in one ad or one commercial, you can have one really good salesperson talk and close a sale with perhaps a million people. It's a tremendous leverage when it's done right, it can build wealth very rapidly. And so I know that some of you know this story, and yet maybe we don't know it as well as we think, because even if we knew the definition of multiplied salesmanship, really what is salesmanship? The nature of salesmanship, and now this is where we're going to start taking this into a new, a whole new level of understanding of how to get breakthrough results in our packages today, in our space ads and any other direct marketing that we do, because we've been sort of coasting the advertising agency business and copywriters of all types who apply this philosophy have been coasting on the coattails of this definition for 100 years. But over the last 100 years, things have changed radically. What was salesmanship back in the days of Hopkins and Lasker is much different than it is today. I remember reading books from salespeople written back in those early days, you know, say Elmer Wheeler was a great sales philosopher, he says, sell the sizzle, never the steak. And there's one of my favorite titles, timid salesmen have skinny kids. And in a sense that's still true, but it sort of described a method of salesmanship

## [27:40 - 30:40]

that was sort of very manipulative and very high pressure, you know, sort of like a life insurance agent cornering somebody and when they didn't have any objection left, you had to sign the policy to send the guy away from your home. You know, Woody Allen's definition of hell is an eternal airline trip seated next to a life insurance agent. But that was the nature of selling it, because I know a lot of people in life insurance have been taught that, you know, there's a quick answer for every objection and you sort of just wear the client out. But we don't have that advantage in advertising because speaking to 10,000 prospects of one to a million, we don't have any power in the situation. We're not in a face-to-face relationship with these folks who are our prospects. So they can just toss our piece in the garbage can instantly or turn the page or just dismiss us without even knowing it. So in a very real sense, salesmanship changes radically when it moves from the person-to-person type of salesmanship and also over the years how our audience has changed because we are so inundated with advertising today that you cannot expect your prospects to be like babes in the woods who have never seen a good sales presentation before. They are processing and trying to avoid sales presentations at all times. And that is a much more powerful factor than it ever was. A hundred years ago, even 50 years ago, when John Capels and David Ogilvy were teaching us how to sell, we have a much, we face a much different situation today because of the tremendous tidal wave of marketing that everyone is hurling at us as prospects, as consumers. So I think if we want to think about salesmanship now, how do we apply, how do we understand what salesmanship is? And to me, I want to simplify everything so easily and perfectly that we can walk out of this room being very good salespeople and knowing what it takes to be a good salesperson, imprint or in person. So let's divide all of salesmanship into two categories. And it's very simple. Opening the sale, starting the conversation with our prospects, and then closing the sale, coming home with the order. And there are different skills in each one of those two. So let's take a look now at the two overreaching categories of skills in all of salesmanship. If you can get really good at opening the sale with your prospects and then taking home the order, that's all you need to know. So let's focus first on opening the sale. Now to do this, I'm going to start each of these major sections with a story. In this case, a very interesting story about a man known as Captain John Raid. Nothing to do with advertising except that he's a master fisherman.

## [30:40 - 33:40]

Pauline and I have for many years owned a home at an Amagansid. Those of you not from New York, I'll explain it. So the Long Island East sticks out like a long finger into the Atlantic. And as such, it's a great, the very tip of Long Island is one of the world-class fishing grounds. Because when fish migrate, they get into this big piece of land, they come around the point of Long Island, which is known as Montauk Point. And many of the fishing world records come from the boats of captains who have caught these fish out of Montauk. So it's a great center of fishing boats, commercial fishing fleets, party fishermen, the sports anglers go out all the time, and then charter boats. It's a very interesting harbor, if you're ever out on the end of Long Island to see Montauk Point. And it's also the home of many legendary fishing captains, including, for example, Frank Mundus, the great white shark hunter who was the model of the captain in Jaws. He's an actual guy and loves to catch great white shark on Rod and Reel. It's his idea of fun, I guess, but... But Captain John... But Captain Frank Mundus, as well as every other fishing captain out there, holds one man in just awe. I mean, he towers above in ability every other captain out there, and they will all readily admit it. And his name is Captain John Raid. In fact, the East Hampton Star, which is the major newspaper of the East End of Long Island, ran a story about him last year. And because he's Captain John here, maestro of Rod and Reel, and you can see the typical days catch these striped bass, these bins that he comes in with. Now, what he is, he's known as a pin hooker, which is the rarest of all type of fishermen on the East End. He is somebody who has a commercial fishing license, which means you don't want all the boats just wiping out the entire schools of fish. So, sports fishermen are allowed only a couple of fish each day, and they have to be a certain size. Commercial fishermen, who fish for food as a business, you're allowed 100 pounds of striped bass and 100 pounds of some other fish, if it's some of flounder or whatever. John Raid is a commercial fisherman, which means he's allowed to catch the hundreds of pounds of fish per day, a different species, and depending on the year, you have a different limit. So, he goes out with a rod and reel. Now, he doesn't do this with nets, like the other commercial fishermen do. No nets, no giant trawlers with those side beams or whatever they are, no big crews of five or six fishermen. He goes out on a little putt-putt motorboat with a rod and reel, comes home with hundreds and hundreds of pounds of fish. So, from a different perspective, on a given Saturday in the summer, there's a Viking starship, which is a big boat that's called a party boat where anybody can come, any angler, sports fishermen come, and so they'll take out 50, 60 fishermen on a Saturday. He will out fish all 50, 60 fishermen by a long shot in the same water.

## [33:40 - 36:40]

So, they ask him, Captain John, how do you do this? I mean, this is an astonishing feat, and you do it every day. Not just when a fish abiding. He will come in almost every day with his limit. And he said something that is profoundly important to us as marketers. When they asked him, Captain John, how do you do this? He thought for a moment, and you can see he's getting up in years, and he's been a fisherman since he was just a child, and he said, when most fishermen go out, and I'd like you to substitute the word marketer of a fisherman, and you'll get this message right away. When most fishermen go out to fish, they're thinking like fishermen. When I go out to fish, I think like a fish. And his whole life has been devoted to how can I think more like a fish. And as marketers, how often do we think like marketers and almost forget what it feels like to be our prospect? And so, he'll tell you that in a typical bait and tackle store, the fisherman goes in and wants to buy every feted lure and fancy rod and reel. But he said most of that is designed to catch fishermen, not fish. And he will study everything there is to know about fish, and what their life is like when they're hungry, when the seasons change, when what bait is running in the water. In fact, the most important thing that he studies is not even the fish itself, but the ocean, because it's the ocean that the fish swim in that determines the temperature of the water, the currents, what bait is running. And by knowing the conditions of the ocean, he has a much better feeling for how to catch a lot more fish. And if we apply that same thinking, we start to see a lot of different things going on with our prospects than if we think of ourselves only as marketers. And if we learn to do this during these next two days, if you can start thinking more like the fish and not so much as a fisherman, I've tried to find a non-sexist way to say fisherman, it just doesn't work. In fact, Captain Linda Greenlaw, who was the captain who avoided the perfect storm, up in that true story about the perfect storm, when somebody on the reporter asked her, should I call you fisherman? I should call you a fish woman. In other words, she says, no, I'm a fisherman and I'm proud of it. That's what you should call me. If it's good enough for Captain Linda, it's good enough for me. But if we can learn to apply this thinking to our marketing, this one idea will prove so valuable to you over the course of your marketing life, to think more like a fish, not so much like a fisherman. And if you do, the rewards are tremendous because persuasion is really the most rewarding activity in a free society. And there's a reason for that because Plato, the great philosopher, said, when you see a lot of advertising or persuasion taking place, and he called it advertising,

## [36:40 - 39:40]

he says it's a sign of a free society because there are only two ways to persuade somebody to do what you want, by force or by persuasion. And so wherever there's a lot of persuasion going on, you know you have a free society because the people there aren't allowed to force each other to do what they want done. And that's why we smiled in the Godfather movie when the Godfather says, I made him an offer he couldn't refuse. That's a phrase that we typically use for persuasive ability, and of course he was using force, which is not allowed. But if you do practice persuasion, as we will be discussing, the rewards are enormous, and you already know this, but enjoy wealth, independence, fame, security, you have a lot of fun doing it, you can pursue any passion you want, you can have the power to help anyone. When you know how to persuade people that there's a cause that's dear to your heart, that gives you a great ability to bring other people to your way of thinking. But you have a great obstacle in all of this, as I was getting to before, how the nature of salesmanship has changed. All of these wonderful rewards can be yours if you can get around a certain obstacle. And the obstacle is not normally what we think, it's not weak headlines, it's not offers that could be stronger, it's not a better mailing list. All of those things are the things that we think about as marketers. When we think of the environment, the ocean that our prospects travel in, the biggest obstacle they have, the prospects have to giving you what you want, can be summed up in one word, and that word is clutter. Because if you think about it, back when Albert Lasker and Claude Hopkins and Johnny Kennedy were writing, there was very little clutter. Nobody knew what advertising was. I mean, they might put up, you know, a product for sale type of advertising, but there wasn't the inundation of marketing messages that we have today. So even 50 years ago, David Algarve and John Capels wrote the rules of good direct marketing. They didn't address this point so much because they didn't experience it. So we had to change our strategy a little bit to cope with this modern world where, believe it or not, somebody did a study, every one of us has 3,000 advertising messages being to us every day, whether commercials, I mean, for every man and woman in America, and it's true in other industrial societies, there were 3,000 people trying to get us to hear their message, whether in magazines or on the radio, TV, and you might say, well, I don't read or listen to 3,000 messages a day. Of course you're not. You're behaving just like your prospects, and that means that you're tossing much of your direct mail unopened. You're deleting your email with a very quick trigger finger. You're letting your magazines and newspapers pile up in a corner of your desk or in the room somewhere in your office, and I know you're doing this. I know on the TV commercials you're running either to the fridge or the bathroom.

## [39:40 - 42:40]

Am I right? And in fact, during the Super Bowl, people have to water authority in various municipalities without having any TV present. They know exactly when the Super Bowl commercials are running because there's a spike in water usage as we're all flushing at the same time, and it goes like this. And so here's the real nexus of the problem. As marketers, we sit and we craft our message so carefully, we bounce it back and forth to the people who help us put it together, polish up the design, we think twice about the offer, and we put it out there to our prospects. This is if we're playing a nice game of catch with our prospects. And here's the problem. Because of all this clutter and the habits that we have to get into to chop our way through, to avoid 3,000 messages a day, as marketers we're playing catch, our prospects are playing dodgeball. They don't want to hear our messages, and they're trying to run from them at every opportunity. And I don't have to really convince you of this as marketers, because if you put on your other hat as consumers, you know what I'm talking about. When you open your email, when you get back from this conference, and you open your email, are you going to be thrilled to see 158 emails? Probably a lot of them from the other people in this room. You're just going to go through and almost automatically, click, click, click, click, click, just, you're not even going to pay attention to this carefully crafted headline and offer and body copy and so on. It's not happening. So it would be almost as if when you walk out of this building, somebody comes up to you and says, can you tell me how to get to Grand Central, and you want to be a helpful citizen, and you try to figure it out, and you give them the best directions you can. You walk three feet, and somebody else runs and interrupts you, and you try to help them. Well, if you walked five blocks and were interrupted by 100 people, you take on a whole different attitude, which is why we in New York act the way we do. But this is what happens in advertising messages. There are so many trying to grab your attention that you can't aim 3,000 messages at people, even though they're not sophisticated marketers. They know how to just blow you off on every opportunity, and they have to, to be able to survive getting through their day. And that's the big enemy. Maybe some of the movies they said, our real enemy has not yet presented himself. Well, that's the real enemy has now emerged. You have to find a way to get past this problem, no matter how good your headline is or whatever. So this takes a whole new way of opening a sale. Remember, it's a satellite big picture. What we're doing here is still in the category of how do we open the sale? What is salesmanship? Two parts, opening the sale, and then we're going to see how there's a better way to close the sale. So this is, we're going to get into now a better way to open the sale in light of this tremendous clutter. In fact, there is a five-word master secret for beating clutter, opening the sale and advertising and achieving breakthrough results. The main point is, if everyone is automatically running away from your sales messages,

## [42:40 - 45:40]

then the first rule of thumb is, don't let your advertising look like a sales message. Now, many of the great books will show you control ads and ads that look very much like sales messages. And for many years, I followed those same examples. In fact, in some of those books, I see control packages, whole-of-fame control packages that I know weren't even working anymore and they were beaten by very large numbers by other packages. And if you follow the wrong model, you're going to get the wrong results. So take all of those ads that you see. Everything is good to learn from. You can learn something from anyone and any type of ad. But the best way to learn is see what is appearing in your own mailbox. So a very good way to see what is really working. Sign up for the packages done by Phillips Publishing, Boardroom, Rodale, Agora. These are the major league players in the world of how to do advertising right. And you'll see what keeps appearing in your mailbox. You'll know what's working. And odds are they're light years ahead of what was held up as a great model from two or three years ago. So, but there is nevertheless, you'll see an ongoing principle that's running through the latest and best control packages. And it's been this way for a number of years now since we put this, and we meaning the home marketing community, put this principle into practice, which five word, master's secret for beating clutter, opening a sale, and achieving breakthrough results is to make your advertising itself valuable. This applies in whatever medium that you like to sell in. Because think about it, when somebody faces that, never that big picture of clutter we had before. The job that you're going to face when you get back is just toss. You're not looking to engage a salesperson. When you're looking to clean your desk of all the direct mail and get through your magazines, your first priority, what can I toss? So, if you make your advertising itself valuable, you make it much more difficult for a person to toss it. Even if the same headline, the same offer, if it looks like sales material, it's automatically has one or two strikes against it. So now, let's how do we do that? For example, in space, when you're doing a space ad, it should look like an article. This is something that John Capels and David Olgovy did talk about 40, 50 years ago. In fact, Olgovy did a study. Many of his clients were not direct response clients. They were package goods and so on. So he relied on readership studies to see what kind of responses he had as we're getting. And he found consistently that ads that look like editorial articles pull or get readership at 500% the rate of ads that look like ads. So right off the bat, you can get a 500% increase in your readership of your advertising by making it look like a valuable editorial article. I know many of you, again, I know many of you are familiar with these principles, but we're going to build on this to take it to a much higher level soon.

## [45:40 - 48:40]

John Capels ran a split run test once and ran the same ad word for word, headlined everything as an ad and then as an article in the readers' digest. And this is in actual orders received, the one that looked like an article pulled in 81% higher sales. And so this is a proven principle even more so today when people have a much quicker trigger finger on getting rid of anything that looks like sales messages. Here's an example. When I was at the Olga V agency in the 1970s, one of our dear friends was a list broker, Walter and Joyce Lake, and they said, Gary, you know, can you help us write an ad in Direct Marketing Magazine to help us get business? And I wrote this ad for them. It looked exactly like an article in Direct Marketing Magazine. They got a lot of business out of this. But it was advice on how to pick a list broker. Olga V himself did an ad. I'm just going to give you a couple of quick examples of space ads that look too valuable to throw away because they either look like articles or they have valuable information. Here's one that Olga V did. A lot of people like to have oysters with their beer over the summer. So Olga V ran a Guinness Guide to Oysters. And that's an ad that you'd want to read. And in the ad, he told why Guinness goes so well with each variety of oysters. So this is not just a buy my beer type of ad. This is giving very good information about if you're an oyster and beer fan, you'll pay a lot of attention to that. And you'll probably want to say, well, maybe I should try Guinness the next time I'm enjoying oysters down in Maryland. Wherever it is. You can see the headline a little more clearly. Some of these ads, they might be hard to see the headline. So I put them in the whole ad and then close up on the headline. Here's another one. How to take out stains. This is an ad for Rinseaux soap detergent. It was a brand-new ad that I ran many years ago. And it showed every type of stain and then directions on the side, on the right, of how to get out that exact stain with his grease, oil, grass, lipstick, whatever, with Rinseaux soap. This ad, despite thousands of other ads running on soap and detergent, this was read and remembered by more people than any detergent ad in history. It shows the power of giving information in the advertising itself. It shows the power of giving value so that you're not just perceived as a salesman or saleswoman trying to sell something. You're somebody offering service, offering tips that people will very often cut out and save. I would probably save that because I'm always staining things. Pauline will attest. Olga V. himself, he took 22 years to write this headline and ad because it's very hard for a copywriter to write about himself or herself. It's the immodesty factor, but also everything you say seems to just be bragging. So he realized this principle and he wrote an ad, How to Create Advertising That Cells. And this brought in, I was at the Olga V. agency when these ads were running,

## [48:40 - 51:41]

he wrote a different version, how to write packaged goods advertising that sells, if they wanted to beef up their packaged goods clients, how to write travel advertising that sells. He had a whole series of them. And they were great because he gave away secrets. For example, look at the subhead to this ad. Just imagine that you own a company and you have no idea if your own advertising agency knows anything about what they're doing. And here Olga V. runs an ad that says, Olga V. and Mather has created over $1 billion, $500 million worth of advertising and spent $4 million, $500,000 tracking the results. Here with all the dogmatism, abrevity, a 38 of the things we have learned. And great tips. Almost gives away all of the secrets that he probably wishes most of his art directors and copywriters would follow more religiously. And that's true by the way. When I joined the Olga V. agency, he said nobody should be allowed to have anything to do with advertising in my company unless they read Claude Hopkins book, Scientific Advertising, seven times. And so I was very young. I wanted to be a dutiful new employee. So I went to the library and I saw the last person to take out that book was like seven years ago. So not even at a big agency where you have a master like Olga V. A lot of people just don't seem to think that they need to learn much about advertising because they're so creative. But when you get into direct marketing and your results are measured, of course then the results are there for all to see. This was another one. And we'll move quickly through these. What everyone should know about the stock and bond business. And this ad featured, I think, let's see, 6,540 words. It ran in the New York Times. And it was a buried offer, no coupon. It pulled 10,000 responses from a single ad. And so if somebody ever says long copy doesn't sell, nobody who's going to read all that, well, they got 10,000 responses from that one ad. And they ran it frequently after that. You could build a very large business getting 10,000 responses or so every time you run an ad. And here's one from just from a magazine I just saw the other day, New York Magazine, Better Than Botox, about a cosmetic that can give even better results, according to the claim anyway, as Botox. It's not exactly an article, but it has an editorial feel. One thing that is a very deaf touch is a question mark after the headline. It doesn't scream a benefit that's hype and that you're not sure you can believe it. It's raising the question, is this better than Botox? It also gives you a lot of flexibility in what you can say, because you're not saying that it is, you're just raising the question. But it sure gets the message across very quickly. Okay, how do we move this into direct mail? Well, the number one way that you can ever get a response and attention of your prospect in direct mail is to try a FedEx package. Obviously it's very expensive, but whenever I had to send a proposal to a client

## [51:41 - 54:44]

that I really wanted that client to read in, or we work in fundraising, and if you're ever sending a proposal to a major donor and you want that person to really consider your cause with a major gift, we always send it in with a FedEx envelope. Nothing gets opened more than a FedEx envelope. Again, this is all under the category of how do we open the sale in a way that does not look like salesmanship, sales letter. You have to open a FedEx envelope. There's no way you cannot open that. Also, it might be a lawsuit, it might be, who knows what, you have to open. If you can't afford that, just a personal letter. You have to open that. It doesn't look like it's manufactured by the millions, and so you have to open a letter like that. Now, you might say, thinking about FedEx envelopes or personal letters, no, we can't afford that. That's really way beyond our price point. Are you thinking like marketers still? If your prospects are telling you, I will always open this. You will always get my attention above anything else if you send me a FedEx package. Might it not be worthwhile, at least as a brainstorming exercise, to see if you can get margins big enough in some really super-duper products that your prospects may love and try to use this wonderful vehicle, because the vehicle is much rarer than products are. I mean, if there's one vehicle that will always get opened, and you just write it off because that doesn't fit the products we're selling, maybe the products you're selling has to be adjusted to what your prospects will always open with great interest. Because if we're still thinking like marketers, it would be like saying, well, my fishing pole is too small to catch that big fish over there, so I'll just let him go. Get a new fishing pole. If you have 100,000 names on your house list, there have got to be 5,000 people, I would guess, who tomorrow, with the right sales pitch coming into FedEx envelope, will send you a check for $5,000. So what is that? It's $25 million. Is that worth exploring? Could we ever come up with whatever we are now, whatever product, could we ever come up with a product that could sell for $5,000 to a very small exclusive part of our list? What is it worth brainstorming about? Because those people are out there, just because they're buying something inexpensive from you now, does not mean that they don't have a lot of money that they would give you if you gave them a good enough presentation, product, and sales pitch. So, and this is the same true as that for personal letters. Don't adjust the size of what you're asking for to the usual way that you've been doing things, because that's thinking like a marketer and not like the prospect, thinking like the fisherman, not the fish. Another way to do this is to apply what I call the crack-a-jack secret, where nobody in the history of the world, as far as I can tell, has ever thrown away a crack-a-jack box without first rummaging to get the little prize inside, even though it's worth maybe half a penny, whatever. So, we can do that in direct mail. And the main way that we started doing this, you know, when we, some of us realized this technique, it's always probably been a technique around for a long time,

## [54:44 - 57:44]

is to give a very good piece, I don't mean a plastic trinket or something gimmicky, the best thing you can give away in a direct mail package is information of great value to your client. So, I once had it write a direct mail package for a survivalist newsletter. A newsletter to go into people who really like to get away from the cities in case a nuclear holocaust is coming, whatever, is a very strong segment of those people out there. And we gave them a survival checklist and closed this with a very big winner. And if you're into that market, if you're passionate about that feeling, you could not throw that away. So, if you're tossing your mail out, you will not toss that out if you're a survivalist, and they're really into checklists, how much, how many tuna fish do I have in the basement and so on. You know, you can't throw that away. So, that's the goal here, is to make your advertising too valuable to throw away. And generally in mag logs and other vehicles, book logs, you may go as high as 50% of your selling space, of your real estate, to give away great information and 50% to sell, not a bad ratio to shoot for. Don't just...after we got going with this principle, and we started seeing control after control was being beaten by not trying to sell something straight out, but in closing something, we started coming up with lots of different ways to do it. So, for example, is Ed Elliott in the room? Is Ed here? Oh, hi, Ed. So good to see you. Ed Elliott, we're very privileged to have Ed here. Now, he's a head of creative services at Phillips. But many years ago, he was a designer, the co-inventor of the mag log. Now, Ed, imagine if you had patented that somehow, or a copyrighted it. Every mag log, of course, is impossible to copyright because it's just a design concept. But so Ed and Jim, a copyrighted Jim Rutz were the first to come up with this. And the version that I did came after theirs. When I saw the...as soon as I saw the first magle, I said, oh, this is it. In space, we've always been able to emulate editorial articles, but I could never figure out a way to do this in direct mail because a direct mail letter is a direct mail letter. But they did this. They said, no, it can look like a magazine. And so this is one that I did with Vicky Moffat as Vicky here. Oh, hi, Vicky. How are you? And this was just a blockbuster. I mean, this brought people... We made a Richard Band look like sort of Man of the Year on Time. We intentionally took Times Red Border. And I think, Vicky, you got a phone call from lawyers at Times saying, that's our color. What are you doing? So we said, what do you mean that's your color? You can't cut and write a color. And they said, well, we have 300 lawyers who say we can. So we said, all right, it's blue and the next mail link is blue. It's no problem. So it became a blue border next time and it worked almost as well. But it was hottest investment opportunities of the year. And right in the mailing, Richard Band, the editor, described some of these great...

Number one growth stock for the year ahead, U.S. Treasury deal of your life. Great investment ideas that were examples of when you subscribed to his newsletter, this is the kind of information that you're going to be getting. Tremendous success. Here's another one that I had done for boardroom around the same time. In fact, I just got this recently from Brian Kurtz. Where's Brian? Brian, hi, Brian. And this had beaten many, many controls before it. I mean, Gene Schwartz and many of the famous copywriters, Mel Martin. This was the Darwinian evolution up to that moment of the best you can do for Brian Kurtz and boardroom. Okay, compare that to the next package. Here's the envelope that it came in. Using this principle, which when only a few of us were realizing it's tremendous power, we had the whole world to ourselves. It's gotten more difficult now that you have other small copywriters have put onto this. But compared to this, which obviously looks like I'm trying to sell you something, but it's very interesting, compared to this, your little black book enclosed. Now, can you throw this away? I don't know about you, you might, but you kind of wonder who's black book, what is this black book? And then when you got to the inside, this is the piece that you would see. And it was filled right on the cover there. You'll see right in this little booklet, we're going to tell you, the safest seat on an airplane and the scary reason why. And these are all of these very intriguing secrets were printed right on the front cover. And you'll know that you could go right into this book. This is immediate gratification. It's not saying as the old school of advertising did, big benefit, send away for the book and you get the answer. Right now, open this booklet and you've got some really interesting information that you're going to discover. Media gratification. And then those are used as examples throughout of what can come in the future if you subscribe to Bottom Line Personal. So this now became, this really is, even to this day, whatever design that you use, state of the art in terms of getting highest response. You must give something away that's valuable. It will almost always out pull the fact this very powerful control.

Boardroom system uses an index system and just to make it simple, the control is always called the 100 index. And this very strong written by, I'm sure, great copyright, I'm not sure who, but beaten form packages by Gene Schwartz and Mel Martin great copywriters. This black book indexed at 233, which means it was 133% stronger than the strongest thing that had gone before. So that's how, what a big, that clutter has made. Toss, toss, toss. Oh, how can I get rid of a little black book? Who knows? Maybe this is personal about me. Maybe it's my black book. Who knows? You have to open that up and then you see these intriguing secrets and they're hard to get rid of also. So then, for example, for Rodale, we did another, I did another, I wrote a package for a book on home remedies. Take the sting out of a bee bite with meat tenderizer. These are things you can't throw away. A much higher percentage of your market will not throw them away. Okay, so just moving along, the principle, we've applied it again and again to lose the weight, just repage it. This was a Rodale book that figured that instead of using the same 6x9 style that many writers use, I said, well, can we borrow the mass head of your sister publication prevention and then, because it has much greater credibility, and sell the book with this kind of a story and did quite well. For Phillips, MDs digest, filled with arthritis breakthrough, pain breakthrough, heart disease breakthrough and so on. What this is, I was thinking, well, you've got to have something that's, something that's valuable, people keep it and open it. Now, most of our prospects are, if they're on the list, it shows that they've bought things before. And so I very often will send away for something and it arrives,

## [1:03:47 - 1:06:50]

I'll look at it, my first impulse is, did I buy this? I better look at it. I'm not sure if it looks like a premium booklet. And that's what this is designed to do. Just like the thing that you get after you subscribed. And so this says, the Trojan Horse, no, it's supposed to say that. It says the Solid Gold Treasury of Inside Information. That was my title, the Trojan Horse Welcome Package. And the subhead there says, a one-volume gold mine of the best secrets ever published in Bottom Line, America's number one magazine of Inside Information. Your free welcome bonus from Bottom Line. Oh, welcome bonus. Did I send this? Are they going to bill me for something? You have to look, you have to see what's going on. But the interior is designed to look like a beautiful premium that you receive after you've signed up. So the Trojan Horse Welcome Package. And this works extremely well. At least the sample that I remember. Almost every time we did it, it was a big winner. Against very strong mag logs and other types of packages. Here's another example of that same principle. It's very subtle. It doesn't scream lots of bullets. It just says, special report and close. That's been used a lot now because this was done a number of years ago and it's been copied. But look at the inside. Again, this will give you an edge over people who are just going this far. The inside of the package is another very glossy looking, just like a premium that you might get when you do subscribe. And it says, this is kind of even sneakier. And so you're allowing the reader, and this is why I say it can be sneakier. It's totally ethical. There's nothing you're not saying, anything that's not true. But you're allowing somebody, reading the headline, just getting past that I got to toss it out syndrome. Because then in a copy, it says something like, you know, I put 38 of my best ideas into this report to show you. I better, maybe I pay for this, I better read it. So you do get past the massacre at the tossing stage, you survive that cycle. And then the inside, the acquisition piece looks just like a premium. Hands off Washington, 38 ideas in 16 minutes on how to get big government out of your wallet and off of your back. And he shares many of his best ideas with many of them saying,

## [1:06:50 - 1:09:52]

well, for completely deals, I have to, you have to subscribe and I'll give you, but I just wanted to give you the overview. And so I'm going to wrap up on this soon. This is what's come to these names. When Vicki and I did this first magologue together, Vicki said, what should we call this format? You know, the production people need a name. And I said, coined a rather ugly name, a magologue. I said, it looks like a magazine, but it sells like a catalog. Why don't we call it a magologue? And that's how that got named. I think really Jim Ruts and Elliot probably should have named it. They invented it. But that's how it got named. And this became, and then looking like a book became a book log. And this unfortunately has become known as an issue log, which is massacring the English language. But this is a newsletter, this is selling a newsletter and it looks just like a newsletter inside. And you mentioned some of the important things that are covered in the enclosed report, enclosed bulletin. Okay, same thing applied here for commodity option newsletter. You can make it look like something valuable. You'll sell a lot more. When Pauline and I bought a home in Garden City, the man who came to insure the home said, what do you do for living? And so what I've learned to say is, and copyrighters who are looking for business, this is a great tool if you're looking to have other practices, a part of your practice, you just say, do you know how every business likes to get new business? And you'll see the question is eyes light up. Oh, yeah. He said, well, I know everything there is to know about how to get new business. And so as soon as I said that to him, kind of just to answer his question, he said, can you get new business for me? So I had a sort of little gleam in my eye as well, because we had just bought this big home and had a big mortgage with it. And I had this idea, there were a lot of local markets. The samples that I've been showing you so far are with the very major national markets, mass marketers rather, you know, Phillips, KCI, boardroom and so on. There are lots of ways you can make money from people who have a local practice. I mean, we have some people in the room who help, one fellow helps other pizza, Paul has made business, he's run a pizza place. We have a dentist who teaches other dentists how to do their marketing properly. And that's true for financial planners. A lot of people who have no idea in your local community how to advertise. And so the gleam in the back of my eye said, you know, he's an insurance agent. I bet you I could create, in fact, I said, well, what is the market you'd most like to crack? And he said, oh, physicians, I could sell them malpractice insurance all day long. So I created this little, I saw, I'll make a deal with you.

## [1:09:52 - 1:12:52]

I'll create a very nice advertising campaign for you. Don't pay me anything, but when it works, I want you to be a testimonial so that when I franchise this across the country to every insurance agent who wants to have a similar business, a similarly effective marketing campaign, you're going to be my testimonial if it works. And he said, that's a great deal. I'll go for that. And that was, and that is a very good way to build wealth. I mean, once you did establish a format that can work for somebody locally, well, then you could just package that and sell it again into a thousand communities across the country. So if you sell something for $5,000 to a local real estate agent in each of a thousand communities, you've just made yourself $5 million for a single piece of copy. So it's leveraged there. It's tremendous. It'll work much better on the internet because I discovered with my insurance broker friend, these folks are not direct male people, so that when I tried to get him to do the list right, he already knew better and he would send it to the wrong list. I gave him a letter. It was a take-off on the Gary Halbert dollar in clothes. Is Gary here, by the way? Oh, there he is. Hi, Gary. How are you? The take-off on the Gary Halbert letter of a dollar in clothes, I try to be a little more creative rather than just swiping Gary's letter. I close a $2 bill with my letter for him. And what he did, see, these are not direct male people and they tend to not listen to these local marketers. They tend to not listen because they think they know better. And what he did is he printed his big letterhead across every page of the letter. And I said, why did you do this? And like many clients, he said, well, look, I did it already. Printing is paid for. I'm going to send it out. So I said, but it's not going to work that way. So he said, well, if it doesn't work, I'll do it again the way you want me to do it. I said, look, I put in a lot of work on this and you're getting a free ride just to be a... You know, I wanted to do this right. So he was... So naturally it didn't work and I said, well, you're going to do it the right way. Well, no, it didn't work. Why should I invest any more money in it? So that's the frustration you run into when you're dealing with local people who are not really good marketing people. But on the Internet, that's a lot easy. You don't have to get them involved with renting mailing lists and doing direct mail production. Those things are very technical and they'll always screw up in these markets that you can franchise. But the opportunity there is tremendous to do that. Who wouldn't want to sell the same piece of copy that's working over and over? And that could be an annuity. I mean, you know, once people see a local real estate broker finds out, you really do bring in great leads and great homes for me to sell. They'll want to spend another $5,000 with you coming year. So if you have a thousand people doing that, you just have a $5 million income from updating a good piece of copy and making sure that it works for them. ## [1:12:52 - 1:15:57]

You know, have to do their own mailings and rent mailing lists. I mentioned the secret of the Wise Old Salesman to reinforce that idea about the Internet. Because one of the best salesmen I ever worked for was a really top producer for Merrill Lynch. He's not far from this building, in fact. He had this midtown territory and boy could he sell. And his secret was, look, I don't try to put heavy pressure on anybody. I'm just incredibly persistent. He says, in fact, it takes me an average of nine phone calls before I will connect with somebody. So he would get a list of top executives and there were many of them in this neck of the woods of Manhattan. And he would call them and say, look, I've got a nice idea. I've done very well with clients at your income bracket. If you'd like, I'll share an idea the next time I have one with you. And usually they'd blow more if the secretary would or assistant would say no thanks. But he would just call back every month. So he said, we take about nine calls finally for somebody to say, he's called. Let me take a message. Who knows what... Persistence has a magical way of making people's all resistance go away. And this is the way it was done in those days when he would be telemarketing. And he would usually get a yes on the ninth phone call sometimes at 12th, but it was worth it in his case. He would call it hunting the elephant because he was going after elephant size clients. People with massive amounts of wealth and if one of them became a client, they would give him commissions and very large amounts. So it was worth it to him. And he said, that's the way I get my business. I'm very successful. You can do that much better on the internet now than you can with telephoning because you're not allowed to telephone anymore, for most cases. But also because it's just much more effective and just through an e-zine, we're offering information. You're not badgering somebody as a pesky salesperson. You're offering value through an e-zine. So if you're not doing an e-zine and using the e-zine as high value, not as just another sales tool, most of the e-zines I get, and I subscribe to a lot of e-zines because I buy almost every product that's out there, I get tons of them and most of them are way overselling. The ratio, like in the magulogs and other information in direct mail, offline advertising, I've always tried to strike a balance. Give away half of your real estate with really great information, the most intriguing, titillating, interesting, valuable information you can find about your product. And save about half of the real estate to sell the next step. I think the ratio is much different online. I think if you follow that ratio, it's still too much selling online. People are so wary of opening emails just to be sold something that is so much different than it was just say two or three years ago.
